mainText:
Good morning. Here's your Breeze communications briefing for Thursday, April 2.

No new Breeze coverage in the last 24 hours.

Now for industry and competitor news.

FlightGlobal reported on April 1st that United, Alaska, and Southwest may all be considering acquiring JetBlue Airways, as the carrier's shares have dropped more than 40 percent since the start of 2025 and the airline has not turned a profit since 2019. If JetBlue is acquired or enters a deeper restructuring, its leisure-heavy East Coast routes — particularly around Boston and Fort Lauderdale — could become accessible to competitors, and network planning should identify where JetBlue's current routes overlap with Breeze's footprint.

C.N.B.C. and multiple outlets reported on April 1st that Delta Air Lines has chosen Amazon's Leo satellite constellation to power high-speed Wi-Fi on 500 aircraft starting in 2028, offering free connectivity to SkyMiles members — a deal that gives Amazon a major win over SpaceX's Starlink. With United, Southwest, and Alaska partnered with Starlink, and now Delta and JetBlue with Amazon Leo, every major U.S. carrier has committed to next-generation satellite Wi-Fi — Breeze leadership should assess where its own connectivity roadmap stands relative to what passengers will soon expect as a baseline.

AirlineGeeks reported on April 1st that Alaska Air Group launched a unified Alaska Hawaiian mobile app combining both brands into a single platform for booking, check-in, and travel management, ahead of a full reservation system migration from Amadeus to Sabre on April 22nd. The Alaska-Hawaiian combination is completing its integration rapidly, and the combined carrier will emerge with a stronger customer-experience platform — Breeze should monitor where the expanded network builds out in leisure markets it already serves.

AeroTime confirmed on April 1st that Alaska Airlines unveiled new fully lie-flat business class suites with privacy doors for its Boeing 787-9 fleet, which will power new long-haul routes to Europe and Asia from Seattle. Post-acquisition Alaska is now building a premium international product that places it in a different competitive tier than before — leadership should track where Alaska's growing network may intersect with Breeze's leisure routes, particularly on the West Coast.

That's your briefing. Roger is standing by when you're ready to dive deeper or take action on any of these stories.
